title: CRL and OCSP Response Freshness and HTTP Status Audits

compliance.assurance_rationale: Auto-migrated from cat-22 markdown on Phase 1.3 run, then normalised in Phase A (clause 'eIDAS' → 'Art.24'). Requires SME review to confirm clause accuracy, mode (satisfies vs detects-violation-of), and assurance level (contributing/partial/full).

dataSources: `index=trust` `pki:ocsp:crl:probe` — trust_service, serial, not_after, ocsp_url, http_status, wallet_id
app: Splunk Enterprise Security (263), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (742)
spl:
index=trust sourcetype="pki:ocsp:crl:probe" earliest=-7d
| eval eidas_tag="crl"
| eval exp_epoch=strptime(not_after,"%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ")
| eval days_to_exp=round((exp_epoch-now())/86400,1)
| stats count as events, min(days_to_exp) as min_days, sum(eval(http_status>=400)) as http_err by trust_service, eidas_tag
| eval uc_id="22.24.14"
| where min_days < 30 OR http_err>0 OR eidas_tag="susp"
| sort min_days
| head 200
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Certificates.All_Certificates by All_Certificates.ssl_subject_common_name, All_Certificates.dest | sort - count
description: Supports qualified trust service auditing, wallet security monitoring, and PKI integrity evidence expected under eIDAS trust frameworks.
value: Supports qualified trust service auditing, wallet security monitoring, and PKI integrity evidence expected under eIDAS trust frameworks.
grandmaExplanation: Supports qualified trust service auditing, wallet security monitoring, and digital certificates integrity evidence expected under eIDAS trust frameworks — so you can show exactly what is being watched and when something went wrong.
implementation: (1) Normalize field extractions and CIM tags; (2) schedule hourly or near-real-time; (3) route alerts to SOC/compliance queues; (4) export evidence packs for supervisory review.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Enterprise Security (263), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (742).
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `index=trust` `pki:ocsp:crl:probe` — trust_service, serial, not_after, ocsp_url, http_status, wallet_id.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Normalize field extractions and CIM tags; (2) schedule hourly or near-real-time; (3) route alerts to SOC/compliance queues; (4) export evidence packs for supervisory review.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=trust sourcetype="pki:ocsp:crl:probe" earliest=-7d
| eval eidas_tag="crl"
| eval exp_epoch=strptime(not_after,"%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ")
| eval days_to_exp=round((exp_epoch-now())/86400,1)
| stats count as events, min(days_to_exp) as min_days, sum(eval(http_status>=400)) as http_err by trust_service, eidas_tag
| eval uc_id="22.24.14"
| where min_days < 30 OR http_err>0 OR eidas_tag="susp"
| sort min_days
| head 200
```

Understanding this SPL

**CRL and OCSP Response Freshness and HTTP Status Audits (eIDAS)** — Supports qualified trust service auditing, wallet security monitoring, and PKI integrity evidence expected under eIDAS trust frameworks.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=trust` `pki:ocsp:crl:probe` — trust_service, serial, not_after, ocsp_url, http_status, wallet_id. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Enterprise Security (263), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (742). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: trust; **sourcetype**: pki:ocsp:crl:probe. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=trust, sourcetype="pki:ocsp:crl:probe", time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **eidas_tag** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **exp_epoch** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **days_to_exp** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by trust_service, eidas_tag** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions (useful for per-host, per-user, or per-entity comparisons for this use case).
• `eval` defines or adjusts **uc_id** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• Filters the current rows with `where min_days < 30 OR http_err>0 OR eidas_tag="susp"` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.
• Limits the number of rows with `head`.

Optional CIM / accelerated variant (same use case, normalized fields via Common Information Model):

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Certificates.All_Certificates by All_Certificates.ssl_subject_common_name, All_Certificates.dest | sort - count
```

Understanding this CIM / accelerated SPL

**CRL and OCSP Response Freshness and HTTP Status Audits (eIDAS)** — Supports qualified trust service auditing, wallet security monitoring, and PKI integrity evidence expected under eIDAS trust frameworks.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=trust` `pki:ocsp:crl:probe` — trust_service, serial, not_after, ocsp_url, http_status, wallet_id. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Enterprise Security (263), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (742). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

This **CIM or accelerated** block uses normalized field names and/or `tstats` over data models. Enable **acceleration** on the referenced models (and correct CIM knowledge objects) or the search may return nothing.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Uses `tstats` against accelerated summaries for data model `Certificates.All_Certificates` — enable acceleration for that model.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Enable Data Model Acceleration (and metric indexes for `mstats`) for the models or datasets referenced above; otherwise `tstats`/`mstats` may return no results from summaries.


Step 3 — Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Single value (min_days), Table (trust_service), Time chart (http_err)
knownFalsePositives: Administrative tasks, scheduled jobs or platform updates can match this pattern — correlate with change management, maintenance windows and user role before raising severity.
